frame_label: Dress Code: Traditional = Respectable, Other = Questionable
generated_comment: Honestly, I think colleges are well within their rights to have a dress code. It's not about restriction, but about upholding some basic 'maryada' and our Indian values. When girls dress appropriately, it looks 'sabhya' and respectable. Unfortunately, some modern clothes can invite unwarranted attention, and it's always better for young women to be safe and avoid putting themselves in such situations. It's for their own good, ultimately.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment covertly links a woman's clothing to her respectability ('sabhya') and subtly blames 'modern clothes' for inviting 'unwarranted attention,' implying that women are responsible for potential harassment based on their attire. This aligns with linking clothing to character and attributing harassment to what a woman was wearing.
original_frame: Dress Code: Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a news article shared on Facebook about a college in Maharashtra that introduced a dress code banning jeans and sleeveless tops for female students. Most commenters are criticising the college. You are writing a comment defending the dress code and explaining why you think institutions have the right and responsibility to regulate how young women dress and what happens in societies where there are no such standards.
